title: True/False/NI: Life as a Wildlife Photographer

tasks.instruction: Przeczytaj ponizszy tekst. Zdecyduj, ktore zdania (1.1.–1.6.) sa zgodne z trescia tekstu (T – True), ktore sa niezgodne (F – False), a ktore zawieraja informacje niepodane w tekscie (NI – No Information). Nastepnie znajdz w tekscie wyrazy lub wyrazenia, ktore odpowiadaja definicjom podanym w punktach 1.7.–1.11. Wymagana jest calkowita poprawnosc ortograficzna odpowiedzi.
tasks.text:
LIFE AS A WILDLIFE PHOTOGRAPHER

People often imagine that my job is one long holiday. They picture me lying on a sunny beach, waiting for a dolphin to leap out of the sea at exactly the right moment. The truth is rather different. Most of my working hours are spent sitting absolutely still inside a small canvas hide, soaked to the skin, watching a patch of empty mud and hoping that something interesting will eventually appear.

I did not plan to become a wildlife photographer. As a teenager I wanted to be a vet, but I was hopeless at chemistry, so that dream quickly faded. Then, on my sixteenth birthday, my grandfather gave me his old camera. I started taking pictures of the birds in our garden, and within a year I was completely hooked. That present really was the turning point in my life.

Patience is the most important quality in this profession. To get a single sharp photograph of an otter, I once waited beside the same river for eleven days. Nothing happened for the first ten. On the morning of the eleventh day, just as I was packing up my equipment, an otter swam straight past me. I had only four seconds to react, but those four seconds were worth every minute of the wait.

The weather is my biggest enemy. Rain can ruin an expensive lens in moments, and bitter cold makes my fingers too numb to press the shutter. I always carry waterproof covers and several pairs of gloves. Even so, I have lost count of the trips that ended early because a storm rolled in.

Despite the discomfort, I would never change my career. There is nothing quite like the thrill of capturing an animal that almost no one has ever photographed before. Each image is a tiny window into a secret world, and I feel lucky to spend my days searching for it.

items.statement: The photographer's daily work is far more glamorous than people assume.
items.explanation: FALSE — the writer says people imagine the job is 'one long holiday', but 'The truth is rather different', and describes sitting still in a hide, 'soaked to the skin', watching empty mud.

items.statement: The photographer originally hoped to work with animals as a vet.
items.explanation: TRUE — 'As a teenager I wanted to be a vet, but I was hopeless at chemistry, so that dream quickly faded.'

items.statement: The camera the grandfather gave was a brand-new model.
items.explanation: FALSE — the writer says the grandfather gave 'his old camera', not a new one.

items.statement: The otter finally appeared while the photographer was getting ready to leave.
items.explanation: TRUE — 'just as I was packing up my equipment, an otter swam straight past me.'

items.statement: The photographer has sold many of the otter pictures to magazines.
items.explanation: NO INFORMATION — the text describes taking the otter photograph but never mentions selling pictures or magazines.

items.statement: Bad weather has sometimes forced the photographer to cut a trip short.
items.explanation: TRUE — 'I have lost count of the trips that ended early because a storm rolled in.'

vocabItems.definition: a small, often hidden shelter from which someone can watch or photograph animals without being seen
a moment when an important change or decision takes place that affects the future
so cold that you can no longer feel something
a strong feeling of excitement and pleasure
the curved piece of glass in a camera that focuses light to form an image
